speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11, 1997. The survivor's name is Morris Rich. My name is Bobbi Kaufman. We're in Miami, Florida, in the United States. The interview will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Today is December 11, 1997. The survivor is Morris Rich. My name is Bobbi Kaufman. We're in Miami, Florida, in the United States. The interview will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All right. Could you please state your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Morris Rich.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe Rutschaisky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-U-T-S-C-H-A-I-S-K-Y, Rutschaisky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Morris, I'd like to explain now, that this is the continuation of an interview we began in August 1996. And after several hours of testimony, we stopped because we all felt that you were uncomfortable and not feeling well enough to continue. And that explains why we're continuing today. Is that accurate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So continue, could you please tell me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born April the 20, 1924, in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what town in Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaunas.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, could you state your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Chaim and my mother's name was Hodel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your siblings, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is Faiga, and my brother Abe, and another sister, Miriam. And one little brother was gassed in Auschwitz. His was, his name was Solomon. And he was killed in Auschwitz when he was four years old with my mother, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to skip way ahead now. When we left off at the previous interview, we were talking about your liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you just briefly summarize again, where you were at liberation and who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was at that time in Bad Tolz. That's a very small town close to the Tirol mountains. And how did I come there to be? Because we were taken out from the main camp, Dachau, and we were chased by foot day and night until we came to that place, Bad Tolz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country is that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in Bavaria. It's not far from Dachau. I don't know, maybe about 30 kilometers. I cannot tell you exactly how many kilometers. But it took us like day and night, two or three nights and day, till we approached Bad Tolz. And there was natural caves there. It was all prepared, all the people to be shot. And when I came to that cave, in Bad Tolz were already a lot of, thousands and thousands of prisoners chased in a day and two days before. And when I was going down in that cave, it was already very late at night. And I actually stumbled on a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a lot of prisoners asked me, where are you coming from? I says, we are from Dachau. And these prisoners says they were from Dachau from different camps, like Dachau Number 4, Dachau Number 2. And one German came to me, a prisoner too. And he spoke to me in German and says, you didn't notice while you were going down that all around that cave is all machine guns lined up with SS? I says, no, it was too dark. I didn't see. But that's the truth. That's what it was. It was prepared everybody to be shot in Bad Tolz in that big cave, you know. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how did you escape from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very difficult situation. I saw then it start snowing and it was very cold. And people are dying every second in the cave there. I start to wandering around and I approached a sewer hole with a big, you know, metal on the top. And I figured, if I crawl in, in that sewer hole, it'll lead somewhere else to another opening, so I'll be out. And I slowly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] picked up that grate from that sewer. And I start walking right into the pipes. And it was approximately, maybe about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 200 feet, I start to see light. And there was a small little village, farm village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that led me out right through the pipes to that little village. And I knocked in the door and a German woman, girl, opened up a little the door. She says in German, 'Was wollen sie denn?' I says in German, I said, please let me in. I am hungry. I am soaking wet. I'm freezing. I'm shaking. So she opened up the door and she let me in and she let me right in the stable where the cows were all lined up and she says, hide yourself right underneath, where the cows are eating, you know. And she covered me with hay. As she told me the Americans are five kilometer away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came in that stable, I felt like I'm in paradise because it was so warm from the cows and it was closed. As she came out and gave me a big piece of bread. And in a cup, in a metal cup, she gave me milk, warm milk. And I felt I'm in heaven. I just passed out right under the hay and I fell asleep. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe two, three hours later, she came and she touched me on the shoulder and she said, the Americans are two kilometers from that, from little town. And I fell asleep again. And then I woke up. I heard the door opening up, and boots, like soldier boots, you know, they're going right in. And the girl opened for them the door. And she was talking to him in German. And he said, I want to hide myself here. And she says, OK, whatever he wants. She was scared. He was, he was SS man. And he saw the end is near. So while I was underneath, down with the cows, he went on top. Like, you know, the farms have like a second, you know, barn, like a barn. And I heard he took off the gun. I heard every knock, like on the floor. And he took off his metal, you know, canteen can. And I said to myself, I'm in bad trouble. I have SS man hiding on the barn on top, and I'm underneath. If I just cough or something, he may shoot me. But it didn't happen that way. I was, again, like couldn't hold my head up, hold my eyes open. And I kept on sleeping and snoozing away. And then the girl knocked me, she says, the Americans are here. And then I heard, you know, all the trucks, you know, and all the tanks. And everything is rolling in, you know, rolling in with such a power. And she woke me up. I run outside, a skeleton. I almost couldn't stand on my feet. And I, they saw my uniform. And right away, the Americans came to me. And some of the Americans were, who could speak, you know, like Polish, you know, have come from a Polish, you know, parents. And some could speak, you know, Russian. And a couple could speak Jewish too, you know, and German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was lucky, you know, that a Polish-American came to me and asked me, where are the Nazis? Where are they? I says, one is upstairs. And he run upstairs, and he brought him down. And he said to me, if you want to punch him in the nose, you can. So I gave him just a little knock. I almost broke my skeleton hand. And they took him right away away. And then they were tooken, pictures they were taking, all kinds of photographs, you know, for me. And then the soldiers came, American, and gave me right away chocolate, and pieces of cake, and, and, and cans of beans, they are [LAUGHS] closed in. And I felt, I, I, what's happening? I couldn't believe I'm free. I'm surrounded by the-- all the Americans. He says, where are all the other prisoners? I told him, they are right maybe about 500 feet in a big cave. And some of the jeeps, they roll right away into the cave. And they right away took all them prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Americans still interviewed me in the street. They gave me all the goodies. And I had, and they gave me in a bag, everything and took photographs. And they asked me where I come from. And then a Jewish American came and spoke to me with the broken Jewish, you know, he knew a couple of words.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I couldn't believe it. I was stunned, you know. I thought, what happened? I'm really free? I touched myself. And after I got all those things, he says, you're very weak. Go back where you came from. You came from this stable here. I said, yes. Go back, and sit down there and eat. So I went down and I ate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were liberating that cave. And in that cave was still my brother. He didn't get a chance to go out. So I was worried all the time about my brother. So when all the people were released from that big, thousands of people, they all came to that town. And my brother was, and I was then outside the door of that farm. And my brother came to me. And he had food, too, Americans gave him. I said, Abie, don't give me no more food. I have a whole bag. Come in, in that stable there, in that farm stable. And my brother and I, we were sitting in the stable and eating, eating. We thought we are going to burst. And then we met a lot of, couple of more relatives, you know, from the cave who came out. And we were kissing and hugging. And we were crying like babies. We couldn't believe that we were liberated. And I stood in that stable maybe more than 3/4 of a day. And then the Americans gave us better houses, was further away, in Germans' homes. And we were relocated. A lot of prisoners, the Germans had to take us in. And we stood there, we stayed there for a couple of days. And then we were transferred to Bad Tolz in the military SS school. In Bad Tolz, they had a school for the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the prisoners were most placed in that school there. And all the Jerry SS were arrested. And we were staying there maybe three, or four, or five days, something like that. And we were fed. And a lot of people died right there. They were too far gone, and they died. While they were eating the food, they died. I saw it with my own eyes. And in that school, a couple of days later while I was walking around, I saw it in the libraries there in the SS school, I saw with my own eyes they had the Jewish Bible, the Tanakh, the Gemara. But the Germans were studying, you know, the SS. And I said, what is that thing doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked somebody there from like a superintendent from the building. He says, that was a school for the SS. And they were studying the all literature from the Jews, you know, from the Bible and from all those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language were the books in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The books were in Hebrew. Hebrew, I could read it. So there must have been a lot of SS that studied, who knew, you know, Hebrew, knew all the languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Morris, I have some questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the cave with you, were there men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was mostly men, only men. I didn't see no women, only men.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that cave, I wouldn't exaggerate. It must have been between 50,000 and 60,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said some of your relatives were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was still there. I snuck myself out, but when he wanted to go out, already SS were walking around there, so he didn't have a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else sneak out also from that sewer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but they went in some other places. Dr. Blumberg and his son, they walked out and they walked away, maybe in a different farm house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the woman in the farmhouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I was too confused, even I was too scared to be polite even to ask her name or anything like that. It was survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another question. The photographs that they took of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. The Americans had all cameras, they took it with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any way of thinking where those photographs might be?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. Some American soldiers, if they're alive, they have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever tried to find any of the pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't even approach anybody to know who took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think perhaps they're in a museum somewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a museum or they are maintained by the people, you know, soldiers they keep it for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were in this school house. Were you given clothing or how were you treated there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were changed right away the clothes, different clothes. And we were given baths, you know. And we had a regular bed to sleep in that German, you know, what they call [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were already starting to recuperate, the people who still had a little strength. But some people had to be taken right away, the ambulances.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you look like at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a skeleton.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you weigh?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 67 pounds, close to 70, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe about five, six days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were transferred in Munich in a certain camp. And then from that camp, they start to send out people in other places, you know, like a transfer place near Munich. And from that place, they took me, I got, I became, very ill with high fever. And they took me to Schwabing Hospital in Munich. It was a hospital named Schwabing. And they took me there because I had a torn lung from pleurisy. My lung busted, and it was rubbing inside in my ribs, and had high fever. So they took me to Schwabing. And I was in Schwabing I think maybe about two weeks. And all the doctors took me to the x-rays and gave me a big injections. And some of the, one doctor, professor in the x-ray room called in assistant, young, you know, doctors, to see my lung. If it would have taken another three or four days more, my lung would have been blood clot, all blood would come out because it was all, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all, you know, sore from the ribs, you know, was torn, the lung and rubbing on the ribs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember the way he described to the doctors, to the other German doctors, young doctors, you see, that was a bad situation. As they gave me in the hospital, I remember, big injections. It's called, I think, calcium lacticum, very big needles. When I used to get injection, I start to feel heat all over, right to my toes, bottles like that, with injections.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took two weeks. And from when I started to get better, they transferred me to a regular hospital. It's called St. Ottelien. That was actually a choice for missionaries. Catholic missionaries, priests, and monks belong that place, St. Ottelien. But in the war times, they made a hospital. They chased out the monks, you know, and the nuns. And they made for the Germans, a hospital. And then when the Americans came, the SS and all the Germans had to leave. And all the sick liberated prisoners from all the camps were transfer to St. Ottelien. And when I came to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the doctors there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then was already Jewish doctors who were liberated and assistant German doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about a German doctor caring for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German doctors, I was a little at-- I had a little fear, but were all the time together with the Jewish doctor. And I personally knew the Jewish doctor. He was from my country, Lithuanian. And he was one of the biggest doctors, Dr. [? Berman. ?] He was the personal doctor to the Lithuanian president, Antanas Smetona. And he was the personal president to the, to that president from Lithuania. And Dr. [? Berman ?] knew me personal, because when I was in the camp, I was sick. And they couldn't help me, when I got my pleurisy, pleurisy in the camp. And he was as a doctor in the camp. And he came to me in the barrack and he says, Moshe, I don't have no medicine. I don't have nothing. I cannot help you. In Lithuania, in Lithuania, I would have known what to do. But here, I can give you only a pyramidon. Pyramidon is like a aspirin. I feel sorry, he says, maybe God will help you. And when I was liberated and he saw me, he couldn't believe it, that I was alive after being so-- with such a high fever. You see, like in a, like in the United States, 104, 105 in the old thermometer. You know, your pain is like 43 or 44 so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was with me. Then after we're liberated, he were all the time with me. Only he was not with me when I was in the German hospital in Schwabing. So he was already in St. Ottelien.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came out from Schwabing and I went to St. Ottelien, we stayed in that hospital one year. I was in St. Ottelien, taken care by the nuns and the Jewish doctors. And we get all kind of best food, and the best medicine. And God bless America, and thank the American government and the, and all America. Did any Jewish agencies help your?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was the, it was the Joint. At that time was Joint and UNRA. And they gave us care packages. And we opened it up, the care packages, and it was all the goodies inside. And we felt like we're in paradise.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about other family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a lot of people start to come from different camps. And people start to meet people. And regards, oh, I saw your sister. She's near Warsaw in a small little place there in a camp. Oh, I said, which sister did he see, Miriam or Faiga? She says, Faiga. And then another came, [? regard ?] because trucks came with people from all over Poland, from all different around, from the liberated camps, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how we start to find, you know. When people are alive, eventually, you know, you get a regard. When people are dead, sorry, we cannot get no more regards. I couldn't get no regards from my father, and from my mother, and my little brother because my sister Fay told me that to the last minute, she was with my mother when she was taken away with my little brother, Solomon, to the crematorium. Where I was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What became of your sister Miriam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam was liberated, and she was in a camp for a little while in Poland. And from Poland, she didn't know what to do. So she went back to Lithuania where the Russians are. And she felt sorry. And then she find out that we were liberated and we are in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had to sell our house to Lithuanians for almost nothing just to make fare and to pay somebody, you know, there were people, you know, who did, you know, like black market, transferring people. She gave them away all the money. And he transferred, took [INAUDIBLE], made her a passport, a Polish. And he smuggled her out from Lithuania to Poland. And when she was in Poland, was already the Jewish organizations. And from there, she says, do you have relatives? She says, I find out I have my two brother near Munich in a small little place. It's called [? Dorfen ?] [? Markt. ?] And she came there and we met all.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my older sister was in Italy. From Poland, she went to Italy. And I didn't see her till she came from Italy to Israel. And from Israel she came to Canada. And then we met in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop any minute now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
